profile.summary: A Senior UX Researcher plans and runs research that shapes product decisions across multiple initiatives. They choose methods, frame studies, synthesize evidence, and influence design, product, and engineering partners. They often mentor less experienced researchers or designers doing research, improve research practice, and help teams connect user insight to roadmap and delivery choices.

known_challenges.text: Getting teams to act on research instead of treating it as a box to tick.
Balancing fast tactical studies with deeper strategic work across several product areas.
Defending research quality when timelines, sample access, or scope are constrained.
Carrying the emotional load of hearing repeated user pain while staying objective and useful.
Influencing product direction without direct authority over roadmap decisions.

recommended_question_themes.why: This role is judged less by activity and more by whether insight changes priorities, design, or delivery.
Senior researchers often juggle urgent requests, strategic work, and partner expectations at the same time.
Strong relationships with product, design, data, and engineering determine whether research lands well.
If all time goes to quick turnaround work, the team misses bigger patterns and future risks.
Frequent context switching, stakeholder pressure, and exposure to user frustration can wear this role down.
Senior level often includes mentoring others and improving how research is done beyond their own studies.

terminology.meaning: Early research to understand needs, problems, and context.
Research that tests a design, flow, or concept.
Structured prompts used to run consistent interviews.
Watching people try tasks to find friction.
Turning raw notes into patterns, insights, and implications.
Shared place for findings, clips, and past studies.
Checking patterns across methods or data sources.
Shared understanding of goals, scope, and decisions needed.
Session or artifact that shares findings and recommendations.
Choosing what research to do given time and impact.
Helping others improve research judgment and execution.
Ongoing learning from users during product development.
Using qualitative and quantitative approaches together.
Finding suitable users for a study.
Open questions and evidence gaps worth investigating.
Review of design work to discuss strengths and risks.

listen_for: Whether they talk about decisions changed, not just studies completed.
How they describe tradeoffs between speed and rigor.
Whether they name partner dynamics as a blocker or an enabler.
Whether they connect findings to roadmap, design choices, or delivery risk.
Shifts from hands-on research detail to coaching, standards, and influence across teams.
avoid: Treating them as a note-taker for design or product instead of a decision-shaping partner.
Pushing only for faster turnaround without discussing research quality and impact.
Assuming senior means they want to stop doing hands-on research.
Reducing success to stakeholder happiness rather than evidence quality and product influence.
